subject: documentation_set
text: [D00032] documentation_set: |  sqlite3 — DB-API 2.0 interface for SQLite databases ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00034] documentation_set: SQLite is a C library that provides a lightweight disk-based database that doesn’t require a separate server process and allows accessing the database using a nonstandard variant of the SQL query language. Some applications can use SQLite for internal data storage. It’s also possible to prototype an application using S...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00035] documentation_set: The sqlite3 module was written by Gerhard Häring. It provides an SQL interface compliant with the DB-API 2.0 specification described by PEP 249 , and requires the third-party SQLite library.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00036] documentation_set: This is an optional module . If it is missing from your copy of CPython, look for documentation from your distributor (that is, whoever provided Python to you). If you are the distributor, see Requirements for optional modules .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00037] documentation_set: This document includes four main sections:  Tutorial teaches how to use the sqlite3 module.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00038] documentation_set: Reference describes the classes and functions this module defines.  How-to guides details how to handle specific tasks.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00039] documentation_set: Explanation provides in-depth background on transaction control.  See also
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00040] documentation_set: https://www.sqlite.org The SQLite web page; the documentation describes the syntax and the available data types for the supported SQL dialect.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00041] documentation_set: https://www.w3schools.com/sql/ Tutorial, reference and examples for learning SQL syntax.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00042] documentation_set: PEP 249 - Database API Specification 2.0 PEP written by Marc-André Lemburg.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00043] documentation_set: Tutorial ¶  In this tutorial, you will create a database of Monty Python movies using basic sqlite3 functionality. It assumes a fundamental understanding of database concepts, including cursors and transactions .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00044] documentation_set: First, we need to create a new database and open a database connection to allow sqlite3 to work with it. Call sqlite3.connect() to create a connection to the database tutorial.db in the current working directory, implicitly creating it if it does not exist:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00047] documentation_set: sqlite3 . connect ( "tutorial.db" )  The returned Connection object con
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00048] documentation_set: represents the connection to the on-disk database.  In order to execute SQL statements and fetch results from SQL queries, we will need to use a database cursor. Call con.cursor() to create the Cursor :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00050] documentation_set: con . cursor ()  Now that we’ve got a database connection and a cursor, we can create a database table movie with columns for title, release year, and review score. For simplicity, we can just use column names in the table declaration – thanks to the flexible typing feature of SQLite, specifying the data types is optio...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00052] documentation_set: We can verify that the new table has been created by querying the sqlite_master table built-in to SQLite, which should now contain an entry for the movie table definition (see The Schema Table for details). Execute that query by calling cur.execute(...) , assign the result to res , and call res.fetchone() to fetch the ...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00055] documentation_set: ('movie',)  We can see that the table has been created, as the query returns a tuple containing the table’s name. If we query sqlite_master for a non-existent table spam ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00056] documentation_set: res.fetchone() will return None :  >>> res
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00060] documentation_set: Now, add two rows of data supplied as SQL literals by executing an INSERT statement, once again by calling cur.execute(...) :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00062] documentation_set: ('Monty Python and the Holy Grail', 1975, 8.2),  ('And Now for Something Completely Different', 1971, 7.5)
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00063] documentation_set: """ )  The INSERT statement implicitly opens a transaction, which needs to be committed before changes are saved in the database (see Transaction control for details). Call con.commit() on the connection object to commit the transaction:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00064] documentation_set: con . commit ()  We can verify that the data was inserted correctly by executing a SELECT query. Use the now-familiar cur.execute(...) to assign the result to res , and call res.fetchall() to return all resulting rows:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00070] documentation_set: [  ( "Monty Python Live at the Hollywood Bowl" ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00072] documentation_set: ( "Monty Python's The Meaning of Life" ,  1983 ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00073] documentation_set: 7.5 ),  ( "Monty Python's Life of Brian" ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00077] documentation_set: # Remember to commit the transaction after executing INSERT.  Notice that ? placeholders are used to bind data to the query. Always use placeholders instead of string formatting
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00078] documentation_set: to bind Python values to SQL statements, to avoid SQL injection attacks
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00080] documentation_set: We can verify that the new rows were inserted by executing a SELECT query, this time iterating over the results of the query:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00084] documentation_set: (1971, 'And Now for Something Completely Different')  (1975, 'Monty Python and the Holy Grail')
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00085] documentation_set: (1979, "Monty Python's Life of Brian")  (1982, 'Monty Python Live at the Hollywood Bowl')
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00086] documentation_set: (1983, "Monty Python's The Meaning of Life")  Each row is a two-item tuple of (year,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00087] documentation_set: title) , matching the columns selected in the query.  Finally, verify that the database has been written to disk by calling con.close()
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00088] documentation_set: to close the existing connection, opening a new one, creating a new cursor, then querying the database:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00096] documentation_set: >>> print ( f 'The highest scoring Monty Python movie is { title !r} , released in { year } ' )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00097] documentation_set: The highest scoring Monty Python movie is 'Monty Python and the Holy Grail', released in 1975
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00098] documentation_set: >>> new_con . close ()  You’ve now created an SQLite database using the sqlite3 module, inserted data and retrieved values from it in multiple ways.
title: documentation_set

text: [D00103] documentation_set: Reference ¶  Module functions ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00104] documentation_set: sqlite3. connect ( database , timeout = 5.0 , detect_types = 0 , isolation_level = 'DEFERRED' , check_same_thread = True , factory = sqlite3.Connection , cached_statements = 128 , uri = False , * , autocommit = sqlite3.LEGACY_TRANSACTION_CONTROL ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00105] documentation_set: Open a connection to an SQLite database.  Parameters :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00106] documentation_set: database ( path-like object ) – The path to the database file to be opened. You can pass ":memory:" to create an SQLite database existing only in memory , and open a connection to it.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00107] documentation_set: timeout ( float ) – How many seconds the connection should wait before raising an OperationalError when a table is locked. If another connection opens a transaction to modify a table, that table will be locked until the transaction is committed. Default five seconds.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00108] documentation_set: detect_types ( int ) – Control whether and how data types not  natively supported by SQLite
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00109] documentation_set: are looked up to be converted to Python types, using the converters registered with register_converter() . Set it to any combination (using | , bitwise or) of
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00110] documentation_set: PARSE_DECLTYPES and PARSE_COLNAMES  to enable this. Column names take precedence over declared types if both flags are set. By default ( 0 ), type detection is disabled.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00111] documentation_set: isolation_level ( str | None ) – Control legacy transaction handling behaviour. See Connection.isolation_level and
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00112] documentation_set: Transaction control via the isolation_level attribute for more information. Can be "DEFERRED" (default), "EXCLUSIVE" or "IMMEDIATE" ; or None to disable opening transactions implicitly. Has no effect unless Connection.autocommit is set to
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00113] documentation_set: LEGACY_TRANSACTION_CONTROL (the default).  check_same_thread ( bool ) – If True (default), ProgrammingError will be raised if the database connection is used by a thread other than the one that created it. If False , the connection may be accessed in multiple threads; write operations may need to be serialized by the u...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00114] documentation_set: factory ( Connection ) – A custom subclass of Connection to create the connection with, if not the default Connection class.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00115] documentation_set: cached_statements ( int ) – The number of statements that sqlite3  should internally cache for this connection, to avoid parsing overhead. By default, 128 statements.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00116] documentation_set: uri ( bool ) – If set to True , database is interpreted as a  URI with a file path and an optional query string. The scheme part must be "file:" , and the path can be relative or absolute. The query string allows passing parameters to SQLite, enabling various How to work with SQLite URIs .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00117] documentation_set: autocommit ( bool ) – Control PEP 249 transaction handling behaviour. See Connection.autocommit and
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00118] documentation_set: Transaction control via the autocommit attribute for more information.  autocommit currently defaults to
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00119] documentation_set: LEGACY_TRANSACTION_CONTROL . The default will change to False in a future Python release.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00120] documentation_set: Return type :  Connection
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00121] documentation_set: Raises an auditing event  sqlite3.connect with argument database .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00122] documentation_set: Raises an auditing event  sqlite3.connect/handle with argument connection_handle .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00123] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.4: Added the uri parameter.  Changed in version 3.7: database can now also be a path-like object , not only a string.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00124] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.10: Added the sqlite3.connect/handle auditing event.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00125] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.12: Added the autocommit parameter.  Changed in version 3.13: Positional use of the parameters timeout , detect_types ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00126] documentation_set: isolation_level , check_same_thread , factory , cached_statements , and uri is deprecated. They will become keyword-only parameters in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00127] documentation_set: sqlite3. complete_statement ( statement ) ¶  Return True if the string statement appears to contain one or more complete SQL statements. No syntactic verification or parsing of any kind is performed, other than checking that there are no unclosed string literals and the statement is terminated by a semicolon.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00130] documentation_set: False  This function may be useful during command-line input to determine if the entered text seems to form a complete SQL statement, or if additional input is needed before calling execute() .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00132] documentation_set: sqlite3. enable_callback_tracebacks ( flag , / ) ¶  Enable or disable callback tracebacks. By default you will not get any tracebacks in user-defined functions, aggregates, converters, authorizer callbacks etc. If you want to debug them, you can call this function with flag set to True . Afterwards, you will get traceb...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00134] documentation_set: Errors in user-defined function callbacks are logged as unraisable exceptions. Use an unraisable
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00135] documentation_set: hook  handler for introspection of the failed callback.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00137] documentation_set: callable to adapt the Python type type  into an SQLite type. The adapter is called with a Python object of type type as its sole argument, and must return a value of a
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00138] documentation_set: type that SQLite natively understands .  sqlite3. register_converter ( typename , converter , / ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00139] documentation_set: Register the converter  callable to convert SQLite objects of type
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00140] documentation_set: typename into a Python object of a specific type. The converter is invoked for all SQLite values of type typename ; it is passed a bytes object and should return an object of the desired Python type. Consult the parameter detect_types of
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00141] documentation_set: connect() for information regarding how type detection works.  Note: typename and the name of the type in your query are matched case-insensitively.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00142] documentation_set: Module constants ¶  sqlite3. LEGACY_TRANSACTION_CONTROL ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00143] documentation_set: Set autocommit to this constant to select old style (pre-Python 3.12) transaction control behaviour. See Transaction control via the isolation_level attribute for more information.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00144] documentation_set: sqlite3. PARSE_DECLTYPES ¶  Pass this flag value to the detect_types parameter of
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00145] documentation_set: connect() to look up a converter function using the declared types for each column. The types are declared when the database table is created.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00146] documentation_set: sqlite3 will look up a converter function using the first word of the declared type as the converter dictionary key. For example:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00165] documentation_set: This flag may be combined with PARSE_COLNAMES using the |  (bitwise or) operator.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00166] documentation_set: Note  Generated fields (for example MAX(p) ) are returned as str . Use PARSE_COLNAMES to enforce types for such queries.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00167] documentation_set: sqlite3. PARSE_COLNAMES ¶  Pass this flag value to the detect_types parameter of
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00168] documentation_set: connect() to look up a converter function by using the type name, parsed from the query column name, as the converter dictionary key. The query column name must be wrapped in double quotes ( " ) and the type name must be wrapped in square brackets ( [] ).
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00175] documentation_set: This flag may be combined with PARSE_DECLTYPES using the |  (bitwise or) operator.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00177] documentation_set: sqlite3. SQLITE_IGNORE ¶  Flags that should be returned by the authorizer_callback
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00178] documentation_set: callable  passed to Connection.set_authorizer() , to indicate whether:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00179] documentation_set: Access is allowed ( SQLITE_OK ),  The SQL statement should be aborted with an error ( SQLITE_DENY )
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00181] documentation_set: String constant stating the supported DB-API level. Required by the DB-API. Hard-coded to "2.0" .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00182] documentation_set: sqlite3. paramstyle ¶  String constant stating the type of parameter marker formatting expected by the sqlite3 module. Required by the DB-API. Hard-coded to
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00184] documentation_set: The named DB-API parameter style is also supported.  sqlite3. sqlite_version ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00186] documentation_set: Version number of the runtime SQLite library as a tuple of  integers .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00187] documentation_set: sqlite3. threadsafety ¶  Integer constant required by the DB-API 2.0, stating the level of thread safety the sqlite3 module supports. This attribute is set based on the default threading mode the underlying SQLite library is compiled with. The SQLite threading modes are:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00188] documentation_set: Single-thread : In this mode, all mutexes are disabled and SQLite is unsafe to use in more than a single thread at once.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00189] documentation_set: Multi-thread : In this mode, SQLite can be safely used by multiple threads provided that no single database connection is used simultaneously in two or more threads.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00190] documentation_set: Serialized : In serialized mode, SQLite can be safely used by multiple threads with no restriction.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00191] documentation_set: The mappings from SQLite threading modes to DB-API 2.0 threadsafety levels are as follows:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00192] documentation_set: SQLite threading mode  threadsafety
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00197] documentation_set: 2  Threads may share the module, but not connections
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00199] documentation_set: 1  Threads may share the module, connections and cursors
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00200] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.11: Set threadsafety dynamically instead of hard-coding it to 1 .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00209] documentation_set: These constants are used for the Connection.setconfig()  and getconfig() methods.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00210] documentation_set: The availability of these constants varies depending on the version of SQLite Python was compiled with.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00213] documentation_set: Deprecated since version 3.12, removed in version 3.14: The version and version_info constants.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00215] documentation_set: sqlite3. Connection ¶  Each open SQLite database is represented by a Connection object, which is created using sqlite3.connect() . Their main purpose is creating Cursor objects, and Transaction control .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00216] documentation_set: See also  How to use connection shortcut methods
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00217] documentation_set: How to use the connection context manager  Changed in version 3.13: A ResourceWarning is emitted if close() is not called before a Connection object is deleted.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00218] documentation_set: An SQLite database connection has the following attributes and methods:  cursor ( factory = Cursor ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00219] documentation_set: Create and return a Cursor object. The cursor method accepts a single optional parameter factory . If supplied, this must be a callable returning an instance of Cursor or its subclasses.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00220] documentation_set: blobopen ( table , column , rowid , / , * , readonly = False , name = 'main' ) ¶  Open a Blob handle to an existing
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00224] documentation_set: rowid ( int ) – The row id where the blob is located.  readonly ( bool ) – Set to True if the blob should be opened without write permissions. Defaults to False .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00225] documentation_set: name ( str ) – The name of the database where the blob is located. Defaults to "main" .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00226] documentation_set: Raises :  OperationalError – When trying to open a blob in a WITHOUT
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00229] documentation_set: The blob size cannot be changed using the Blob class. Use the SQL function zeroblob to create a blob with a fixed size.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00231] documentation_set: Commit any pending transaction to the database. If autocommit is True , or there is no open transaction, this method does nothing. If autocommit is False , a new transaction is implicitly opened if a pending transaction was committed by this method.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00232] documentation_set: rollback ( ) ¶  Roll back to the start of any pending transaction. If autocommit is True , or there is no open transaction, this method does nothing. If autocommit is False , a new transaction is implicitly opened if a pending transaction was rolled back by this method.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00233] documentation_set: close ( ) ¶  Close the database connection. If autocommit is False , any pending transaction is implicitly rolled back. If autocommit is True or LEGACY_TRANSACTION_CONTROL , no implicit transaction control is executed. Make sure to commit() before closing to avoid losing pending changes.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00234] documentation_set: execute ( sql , parameters = () , / ) ¶  Create a new Cursor object and call
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00235] documentation_set: execute() on it with the given sql and parameters . Return the new cursor object.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00236] documentation_set: executemany ( sql , parameters , / ) ¶  Create a new Cursor object and call
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00237] documentation_set: executemany() on it with the given sql and parameters . Return the new cursor object.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00238] documentation_set: executescript ( sql_script , / ) ¶  Create a new Cursor object and call
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00239] documentation_set: executescript() on it with the given sql_script . Return the new cursor object.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00240] documentation_set: create_function ( name , narg , func , * , deterministic = False ) ¶  Create or remove a user-defined SQL function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00241] documentation_set: Parameters :  name ( str ) – The name of the SQL function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00242] documentation_set: narg ( int ) – The number of arguments the SQL function can accept. If -1 , it may take any number of arguments.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00243] documentation_set: func ( callback | None) – A callable that is called when the SQL function is invoked. The callable must return a type natively supported by SQLite . Set to None to remove an existing SQL function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00244] documentation_set: deterministic ( bool ) – If True , the created SQL function is marked as  deterministic , which allows SQLite to perform additional optimizations.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00245] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.8: Added the deterministic parameter.  Example:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00257] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.13: Passing name , narg , and func as keyword arguments is deprecated. These parameters will become positional-only in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00258] documentation_set: create_aggregate ( name , n_arg , aggregate_class ) ¶  Create or remove a user-defined SQL aggregate function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00259] documentation_set: Parameters :  name ( str ) – The name of the SQL aggregate function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00260] documentation_set: n_arg ( int ) – The number of arguments the SQL aggregate function can accept. If -1 , it may take any number of arguments.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00261] documentation_set: aggregate_class ( class | None) – A class must implement the following methods:  step() : Add a row to the aggregate.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00262] documentation_set: finalize() : Return the final result of the aggregate as  a type natively supported by SQLite .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00263] documentation_set: The number of arguments that the step() method must accept is controlled by n_arg .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00264] documentation_set: Set to None to remove an existing SQL aggregate function.  Example:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00280] documentation_set: con . close ()  Changed in version 3.13: Passing name , n_arg , and aggregate_class as keyword arguments is deprecated. These parameters will become positional-only in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00281] documentation_set: create_window_function ( name , num_params , aggregate_class , / ) ¶  Create or remove a user-defined aggregate window function.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00282] documentation_set: Parameters :  name ( str ) – The name of the SQL aggregate window function to create or remove.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00283] documentation_set: num_params ( int ) – The number of arguments the SQL aggregate window function can accept. If -1 , it may take any number of arguments.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00284] documentation_set: aggregate_class ( class | None) – A class that must implement the following methods:  step() : Add a row to the current window.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00285] documentation_set: value() : Return the current value of the aggregate.  inverse() : Remove a row from the current window.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00286] documentation_set: finalize() : Return the final result of the aggregate as  a type natively supported by SQLite .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00287] documentation_set: The number of arguments that the step() and value() methods must accept is controlled by num_params .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00288] documentation_set: Set to None to remove an existing SQL aggregate window function.  Raises :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00289] documentation_set: NotSupportedError – If used with a version of SQLite older than 3.25.0, which does not support aggregate window functions.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00290] documentation_set: Added in version 3.11.  Example:
title: documentation_set

title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00305] documentation_set: finalize ( self ):  """Return the final value of the aggregate.

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00325] documentation_set: Create a collation named name using the collating function callable .  callable is passed two string arguments, and it should return an integer :
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00327] documentation_set: 0 if they are ordered equal  The following example shows a reverse sorting collation:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00346] documentation_set: Remove a collation function by setting callable to None .  Changed in version 3.11: The collation name can contain any Unicode character. Earlier, only ASCII characters were allowed.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00347] documentation_set: interrupt ( ) ¶  Call this method from a different thread to abort any queries that might be executing on the connection. Aborted queries will raise an OperationalError .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00348] documentation_set: set_authorizer ( authorizer_callback ) ¶  Register callable
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00349] documentation_set: authorizer_callback to be invoked for each attempt to access a column of a table in the database. The callback should return one of SQLITE_OK ,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00351] documentation_set: The first argument to the callback signifies what kind of operation is to be authorized. The second and third argument will be arguments or None
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00352] documentation_set: depending on the first argument. The 4th argument is the name of the database (“main”, “temp”, etc.) if applicable. The 5th argument is the name of the inner-most trigger or view that is responsible for the access attempt or
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00353] documentation_set: None if this access attempt is directly from input SQL code.  Please consult the SQLite documentation about the possible values for the first argument and the meaning of the second and third argument depending on the first one. All necessary constants are available in the sqlite3 module.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00354] documentation_set: Passing None as authorizer_callback will disable the authorizer.  Changed in version 3.11: Added support for disabling the authorizer using None .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00355] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.13: Passing authorizer_callback as a keyword argument is deprecated. The parameter will become positional-only in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00356] documentation_set: set_progress_handler ( progress_handler , n ) ¶  Register callable
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00357] documentation_set: progress_handler to be invoked for every n  instructions of the SQLite virtual machine. This is useful if you want to get called from SQLite during long-running operations, for example to update a GUI.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00358] documentation_set: If you want to clear any previously installed progress handler, call the method with None for progress_handler .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00359] documentation_set: Returning a non-zero value from the handler function will terminate the currently executing query and cause it to raise a DatabaseError
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00360] documentation_set: exception.  Changed in version 3.13: Passing progress_handler as a keyword argument is deprecated. The parameter will become positional-only in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00361] documentation_set: set_trace_callback ( trace_callback ) ¶  Register callable
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00362] documentation_set: trace_callback to be invoked for each SQL statement that is actually executed by the SQLite backend.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00363] documentation_set: The only argument passed to the callback is the statement (as  str ) that is being executed. The return value of the callback is ignored. Note that the backend does not only run statements passed to the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00364] documentation_set: Cursor.execute() methods. Other sources include the  transaction management of the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00365] documentation_set: sqlite3 module and the execution of triggers defined in the current database.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00366] documentation_set: Passing None as trace_callback will disable the trace callback.  Note
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00367] documentation_set: Exceptions raised in the trace callback are not propagated. As a development and debugging aid, use
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00368] documentation_set: enable_callback_tracebacks() to enable printing tracebacks from exceptions raised in the trace callback.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00369] documentation_set: Added in version 3.3.  Changed in version 3.13: Passing trace_callback as a keyword argument is deprecated. The parameter will become positional-only in Python 3.15.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00370] documentation_set: enable_load_extension ( enabled , / ) ¶  Enable the SQLite engine to load SQLite extensions from shared libraries if enabled is True ; else, disallow loading SQLite extensions. SQLite extensions can define new functions, aggregates or whole new virtual table implementations. One well-known extension is the fulltext-sea...
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00371] documentation_set: Note  The sqlite3 module is not built with loadable extension support by default, because some platforms (notably macOS) have SQLite libraries which are compiled without this feature. To get loadable extension support, you must pass the --enable-loadable-sqlite-extensions option to configure .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00372] documentation_set: Raises an auditing event  sqlite3.enable_load_extension with arguments connection , enabled .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00373] documentation_set: Added in version 3.2.  Changed in version 3.10: Added the sqlite3.enable_load_extension auditing event.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00374] documentation_set: con . enable_load_extension ( True )  # Load the fulltext search extension
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00375] documentation_set: con . execute ( "select load_extension('./fts3.so')" )  # alternatively you can load the extension using an API call:
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00378] documentation_set: con . execute ( "CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE recipe USING fts3(name, ingredients)" )  con . executescript ( """
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00379] documentation_set: INSERT INTO recipe (name, ingredients) VALUES('broccoli stew', 'broccoli peppers cheese tomatoes');
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00381] documentation_set: INSERT INTO recipe (name, ingredients) VALUES('broccoli pie', 'broccoli cheese onions flour');
title: documentation_set

text: [D00386] documentation_set: print ( row )  load_extension ( path , / , * , entrypoint = None ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00387] documentation_set: Load an SQLite extension from a shared library. Enable extension loading with enable_load_extension() before calling this method.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00388] documentation_set: Parameters :  path ( str ) – The path to the SQLite extension.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00389] documentation_set: entrypoint ( str | None ) – Entry point name. If None (the default), SQLite will come up with an entry point name of its own; see the SQLite docs Loading an Extension for details.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00390] documentation_set: Raises an auditing event  sqlite3.load_extension with arguments connection , path .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00391] documentation_set: Added in version 3.2.  Changed in version 3.10: Added the sqlite3.load_extension auditing event.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00392] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.12: Added the entrypoint parameter.  iterdump ( * , filter = None ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00393] documentation_set: Return an iterator to dump the database as SQL source code. Useful when saving an in-memory database for later restoration. Similar to the .dump command in the sqlite3 shell.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00394] documentation_set: Parameters :  filter ( str | None ) – An optional LIKE pattern for database objects to dump, e.g. prefix_% . If None (the default), all database objects will be included.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00395] documentation_set: Example:  # Convert file example.db to SQL dump file dump.sql
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00405] documentation_set: Changed in version 3.13: Added the filter parameter.  backup ( target , * , pages = -1 , progress = None , name = 'main' , sleep = 0.250 ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00406] documentation_set: Create a backup of an SQLite database.  Works even if the database is being accessed by other clients or concurrently by the same connection.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00407] documentation_set: Parameters :  target ( Connection ) – The database connection to save the backup to.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00408] documentation_set: pages ( int ) – The number of pages to copy at a time. If equal to or less than 0 , the entire database is copied in a single step. Defaults to -1 .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00409] documentation_set: progress ( callback | None) – If set to a callable , it is invoked with three integer arguments for every backup iteration: the status of the last iteration, the remaining number of pages still to be copied, and the total number of pages. Defaults to None .
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00410] documentation_set: name ( str ) – The name of the database to back up. Either "main" (the default) for the main database,
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00411] documentation_set: "temp" for the temporary database, or the name of a custom database as attached using the
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00413] documentation_set: sleep ( float ) – The number of seconds to sleep between successive attempts to back up remaining pages.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00414] documentation_set: Example 1, copy an existing database into another:  def
title: documentation_set

title: documentation_set

title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00433] documentation_set: Raises :  ProgrammingError – If category is not recognised by the underlying SQLite library.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00436] documentation_set: Added in version 3.11.  setlimit ( category , limit , / ) ¶
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00437] documentation_set: Set a connection runtime limit. Attempts to increase a limit above its hard upper bound are silently truncated to the hard upper bound. Regardless of whether or not the limit was changed, the prior value of the limit is returned.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00438] documentation_set: Parameters :  category ( int ) – The SQLite limit category to be set.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00439] documentation_set: limit ( int ) – The value of the new limit. If negative, the current limit is unchanged.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set
text: [D00441] documentation_set: Raises :  ProgrammingError – If category is not recognised by the underlying SQLite library.
title: documentation_set

subject: documentation_set

title: documentation_set
